title: Coffee and walnut cake
subtitle: The British classic: coffee buttercream between two walnut sponge layers
excerpt: Two layers of walnut-studded coffee sponge sandwiched and covered with a strong coffee buttercream, the top decorated with walnut halves. A British tearoom staple made well at home.
sourceNotes: The coffee and walnut cake is a twentieth-century British tea cake documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, and widely reproduced throughout British domestic baking literature from the 1940s onward. The combination of espresso-strength instant coffee with walnut in a creamed butter sponge is distinctly British in style.

content.text: Coffee and walnut cake is a British tearoom constant. The sponge is made by the

with strong coffee and chopped walnuts folded into the batter. The buttercream is coffee-flavoured and bold, this is not a subtle cake. The quality of the coffee flavour depends on using strong, concentrated liquid: two teaspoons of instant coffee dissolved in one tablespoon of boiling water gives a more assertive result than a weak espresso.

The walnuts should be roughly chopped rather than finely ground. You want texture in the crumb as well as in the decoration. The halved walnuts pressed into the top of the finished cake are as much a flavour element as decoration.

attrs.prepNote: softened; plus 150 g for the buttercream
roughly chopped; plus 8 halves for decoration

content.text: Dissolve 3 teaspoons of instant coffee in 3 tablespoons of boiling water. Stir well and leave to cool. This coffee liquid is divided between the sponge and the buttercream.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line two 20 cm round tins. Beat the sponge quantity of {{unsalted-butter}} with {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in {{eggs}} gradually. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}}, half the coffee concentrate, and the chopped {{walnuts}}. Divide equally between the tins. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the tops spring back when pressed and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for five minutes, then on a rack.

Beat the buttercream quantity of {{unsalted-butter}} until very pale and soft. Gradually beat in {{icing-sugar}}, then the remaining coffee concentrate. The buttercream should be smooth, spreadable, and strongly coffee-flavoured. Spread half the buttercream over the top of one cooled sponge layer. Set the second layer on top. Cover the top and sides of the cake with the remaining buttercream, or leave the sides bare and pile the buttercream thickly on top. Press walnut halves into the surface.
Very soft room-temperature butter is essential for smooth buttercream. Cold butter makes a gritty, separated result that cannot be rescued by beating harder.
For a less sweet buttercream, replace up to 50 g of icing sugar with a tablespoon of very strong cold espresso. The buttercream will be a little looser but the coffee flavour more complex.
The cake keeps at room temperature for two days, covered loosely. After that it dries out and should be refrigerated and brought back to room temperature before serving.
